title: Find last occurrence of a target
description: Find the last occurrence index of a target element in a sorted array using binary search

examples.explanation: Start at index 4 (value 4): 4 ≠ 2 → move to index 3 (value 3): 3 ≠ 2 → move to index 2 (value 2): 2 = 2 → found! Return index 2.
Start at index 3 (value 5): 5 = 5 → found immediately! Return index 3. Note: This is the last occurrence since we started at the end.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array from the end to the beginning, returning the first index where the target matches. Use this when simplicity is prioritized over efficiency, as it works on any array without requiring sorted order.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Last Occurrence with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution: Iterate from end to start to find last occurrence of target in any array.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the problem by scanning the array from the last element backward to the first. The intuition is simple: since we want the LAST occurrence, we start at the end where the last occurrence would be located if it exists. By moving backward, the first matching element we encounter will be the last occurrence in the original array. This works on any array (sorted or unsorted) because we're doing a complete linear scan from the end. The algorithm: 1) Start at the last index (n-1). 2) Move backward one index at a time. 3) At each position, check if array[i] equals target. 4) If found, return i immediately. 5) If we reach the beginning without finding target, return -1. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case (target not present or at beginning). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the brute force approach by implementing the complete solution. Common mistakes include: starting from the beginning (which finds first occurrence), not handling empty arrays, or forgetting to return -1 when target not found. The key insight is that backward iteration naturally finds last occurrence without needing to track anything.

examples.trace: Start at index 4 (value 4): 4 ≠ 2 → move to index 3 (value 3): 3 ≠ 2 → move to index 2 (value 2): 2 = 2 → found! Return index 2.
Start at index 3 (value 5): 5 = 5 → found immediately! Return index 3. Note: This is the last occurrence since we started at the end.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backward Linear Search
ladders.desc: The backward iteration that finds matching elements from the end.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core search logic: scanning an array from the last index to the first to find an element matching a target. The intuition is that by starting at the end and moving backward, we naturally encounter the last occurrence first if it exists. This is more efficient than forward search for finding last occurrences because we don't need to scan the entire array when the target exists - we can stop at the first match. The algorithm: 1) Initialize i = n-1 (last index). 2) While i ≥ 0: check if arr[i] == target. 3) If true, return i (this is the last occurrence). 4) Decrement i. 5) If loop completes, return -1. Time: O(n) worst case, O(1) best case (if target at last position). Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the complete search logic - L0 just adds input validation and return handling. Common mistakes: using i > 0 instead of i ≥ 0 (misses first element), not decrementing i, or returning wrong value. The backward direction is key - forward search would require remembering the last match.

examples.trace: i=2: arr[2]=4 = 4 → return immediately (best case)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Last Occurrence Detection via Backward Traversal
ladders.desc: The insight that backward iteration finds last occurrence naturally.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the fundamental insight: to find the LAST occurrence of an element, traverse the array FROM THE END TOWARD THE BEGINNING. The intuition is geometric - imagine the array as a timeline from left (first) to right (last). The last occurrence is at the rightmost position where target appears. By starting at the right end and moving left, the first matching position we hit is guaranteed to be the rightmost/last occurrence. This is more efficient than forward traversal for this specific problem because with forward traversal, you'd need to remember the most recent match and continue scanning. With backward traversal, you can stop at the first match. The algorithm essence: start at last index, move left until you find target or reach beginning. Time: O(n) but with better constants than forward search for finding last occurrences. Space: O(1). This connects to L1 as the conceptual foundation for why the backward search subroutine works. Common mistakes: not understanding why backward is better than forward for this problem, or trying to implement forward search with extra variables. The key is recognizing that search direction should match what you're looking for (first vs last).

examples.input: Visualize: [A, B, B, C, B] find last B
examples.trace: Forward: See A (not B), B at 1 (remember), B at 2 (update), C at 3 (not), B at 4 (update) → return 4 after full scan. Backward: Start at 4 (B) → found immediately! Return 4.

examples.input: Visualize: [X, X, Y, X] find last X
examples.trace: Backward: Index 3 (X) → found immediately. Forward would need to scan all elements and remember last X seen.

testCases.input: Concept: Why backward for last occurrence?

testCases.expected: Target at first index or not present

testCases.input: Forward search for last occurrence needs?

testCases.expected: No need to track, can stop early

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal in Reverse Order
ladders.desc: Iterating through an array from last index to first.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to traverse an array in reverse order, which is essential for the backward search approach. The intuition is that arrays are typically accessed sequentially from first to last (forward), but we can also access them from last to first (backward) by starting at the highest index and decrementing. This is fundamental because many algorithms require reverse processing. The algorithm: 1) Find array length n. 2) Start index i at n-1 (last valid index). 3) While i ≥ 0: access arr[i], then decrement i by 1. 4) Stop when i becomes -1 (before first element). Time: O(n) for full traversal. Space: O(1). This connects to L2 as the mechanical implementation of backward traversal needed for last occurrence detection. Common mistakes: starting at n instead of n-1 (out of bounds), using i > 0 instead of i ≥ 0 (misses index 0), or decrementing incorrectly. The key is understanding array indices: 0-based indexing means valid indices are 0 to n-1, so we start at n-1 and go down to 0 inclusive.

examples.trace: n=1, i=0: arr[0]=5 → i=-1 stop

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access with Index
ladders.desc: Accessing an array element using its index position.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers how to access individual elements in an array using indices, which is fundamental to all array algorithms. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has a numerical index representing its position. In 0-based indexing (used in most languages), the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, up to last at index n-1 for an array of length n. Accessing arr[i] retrieves the element at position i. This operation is O(1) time complexity because arrays provide direct access via memory address calculation. Space is O(1) for the access itself. This connects to L3 as the fundamental operation needed during traversal - at each step in backward traversal, we access arr[i]. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (thinking index 1 is first), accessing out of bounds (i < 0 or i ≥ n), or confusing index with value. The key is understanding that indices are positions, not values, and that valid indices range from 0 to length-1.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. arr[1] gives element at position 1, which is 20.
Single element array: index 0 stores the value 5. arr[0] returns 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array?
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as ordered collections of elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is an ordered collection of elements, all of the same type, stored in contiguous memory locations. The intuition is like a row of lockers: each locker has a number (index) and contains one item (element). The order matters - elements have positions. Key characteristics: 1) Fixed size (usually) when created. 2) 0-based indexing in most languages (first element at position 0). 3) Direct access to any element via its index. 4) Contiguous storage for cache efficiency. This is the absolute prerequisite for the problem because we need an array to search through. Time/space: Creating an array takes O(n) time and O(n) space for n elements. This connects to L4 as the structure we're accessing - L4's element access operates on arrays. Common beginner mistakes: confusing arrays with lists (in Python, lists are dynamic), thinking indices start at 1, or not understanding that arrays store references/values. The key is grasping that arrays provide ordered storage with positional access.

examples.trace: Memory allocates 3 contiguous slots. Slot 0 stores 5, slot 1 stores 10, slot 2 stores 15. Length is 3.
No memory allocated for elements. Length is 0. No valid indices.

approaches.name: Binary Search with Post-Processing O(log n)
approaches.summary: Perform a standard binary search to find any occurrence of the target, then scan linearly to the right to find the last occurrence. This is efficient for sorted arrays but can degrade to O(n) if many duplicates exist.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Last Occurrence with Binary Search and Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search to find any target occurrence, then scanning right to find the last occurrence.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the sorted nature of the array to quickly locate a target element using binary search, then performs a linear scan to find the last occurrence. The intuition is that binary search gives us O(log n) access to any target element, and since the array is sorted, all duplicates will be contiguous. After finding one occurrence, we can simply scan rightwards until we find a different element or reach the end. The algorithm: 1. Perform standard binary search to find any index where arr[mid] == target. 2. If found, initialize last_index = mid. 3. While last_index + 1 < len(arr) and arr[last_index + 1] == target: increment last_index. 4. Return last_index. If not found, return -1. Time complexity is O(log n) for binary search plus O(k) for scanning duplicates, where k is the number of duplicates after the found index. In worst case (all elements are target), this degrades to O(n). Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the binary search subroutine as the starting point. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, off-by-one errors in the scan, or returning the first found index without scanning. Real code pattern: def find_last_occurrence(arr, target): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; found_index = -1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: found_index = mid; break; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; if found_index != -1: while found_index+1 < len(arr) and arr[found_index+1] == target: found_index += 1; return found_index.

examples.trace: Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2, arr[2]=2 == target, found_index=2. Linear scan: found_index=2, check arr[3]=2 == target, increment to 3; check arr[4]=3 != target, stop. Return 3.
Binary search: low=0, high=3, mid=1, arr[1]=3 < 4, low=2; mid=2, arr[2]=5 > 4, high=1; low=2 > high=1, loop ends, found_index=-1. Return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Binary Search to Find Any Target Occurrence
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to locate any index where the target element exists in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the approach, providing O(log n) access to the target. The intuition is that in a sorted array, we can repeatedly halve the search space by comparing the middle element to the target. If the middle equals the target, we've found an occurrence. If it's less, the target must be in the right half; if greater, in the left half. Algorithm: 1. Initialize low=0, high=len(arr)-1. 2. While low <= high: a. Compute mid = (low+high)//2. b. If arr[mid] == target, return mid (found). c. If arr[mid] < target, set low = mid+1. d. Else, set high = mid-1. 3. Return -1 (not found). Time: O(log n), space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by giving a starting index for the linear scan. Common mistakes include infinite loops from incorrect termination conditions (use low <= high), integer overflow in mid calculation (use low + (high-low)//2), or not updating bounds correctly. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: low=0, high=4, mid=2, arr[2]=3 == target, return 2.
low=0, high=3, mid=1, arr[1]=20 < 25, low=2; mid=2, arr[2]=30 > 25, high=1; low=2 > high=1, loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Linear Scan to Find Last Duplicate in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given a starting index in a sorted array, scan rightwards to find the last duplicate element.
ladders.explanation: This technique exploits the property that in a sorted array, duplicate elements are contiguous. Once we have an index where the target exists (from binary search), we can efficiently find the last occurrence by scanning linearly to the right until we encounter a different element or reach the end. The intuition is simple: since the array is sorted, all duplicates of the target will be grouped together, so the last one will be at the highest index where the value still equals the target. Algorithm: 1. Start with index i (where arr[i] == target). 2. While i+1 < len(arr) and arr[i+1] == target: increment i by 1. 3. Return i. Time: O(k) where k is the number of duplicates after the starting index, O(n) in worst case. Space: O(1). This connects to L0 by completing the solution after binary search. Common mistakes include not checking array bounds (i+1 < len(arr)) to avoid index errors, or scanning left instead of right (which would find the first occurrence). Real code pattern: def find_last_from_index(arr, start_idx, target): i = start_idx; while i+1 < len(arr) and arr[i+1] == target: i += 1; return i.

examples.trace: i=1, check arr[2]=2 == target, i=2; check arr[3]=2 == target, i=3; check arr[4]=3 != target, stop. Return 3.
i=0, check arr[1]=5 == target, i=1; check arr[2]=5 == target, i=2; i+1=3 >= len(arr), stop. Return 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through an Array with Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Traverse array elements sequentially while ensuring index stays within valid bounds.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves looping through an array from a given starting point to find a condition, with careful attention to array bounds to prevent errors. The intuition is that arrays have fixed sizes, and accessing indices outside the range [0, len(arr)-1] causes runtime errors. When scanning, we must check before each access that the index is valid. Algorithm: 1. Start at index i. 2. While i < len(arr) and some_condition(arr[i]): process arr[i], then increment i. Alternatively, for scanning right from a start: while i+1 < len(arr) and arr[i+1] meets condition: increment i. Time: O(n) in worst case, space: O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism for the linear scan. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using i <= len(arr) instead of i < len(arr)), or not checking bounds before access (leading to IndexError). Real code pattern: def scan_array(arr, start_idx): i = start_idx; while i < len(arr): # process arr[i]; i += 1.

examples.trace: i=0, i<4 true, process arr[0]=1; i=1, process arr[1]=2; i=2, process arr[2]=3; i=3, process arr[3]=4; i=4, i<4 false, stop.
i=1, i<2 true, process arr[1]=6; i=2, i<2 false, stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements with a Target Value
ladders.desc: Check if an element at a given index in an array equals a specified target value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an array element and comparing it to a target value using equality operator. The intuition is straightforward: arrays store elements at indices, and we can retrieve and compare them. Steps: 1. Ensure index i is within bounds: 0 <= i < len(arr). 2. Access arr[i] using array indexing. 3. Compare arr[i] == target, which returns True if equal, False otherwise. Time: O(1), space: O(1). This connects to L3 as the condition checked during iteration. Common mistakes include comparing without bounds check (risking IndexError), or using assignment (=) instead of equality (==). Real code pattern: if i < len(arr) and arr[i] == target: # do something.

examples.trace: Check i=1 < len(arr)=3, access arr[1]=10, compare 10 == 10, result True.
Check i=0 < len(arr)=3, access arr[0]=1, compare 1 == 5, result False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is, how to access elements by index, and basic properties.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. The intuition is like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0: you can put items in the boxes and retrieve them using their box number. Key concepts: 1. Indexing: arr[i] accesses the element at position i, where i is an integer from 0 to len(arr)-1. 2. Length: len(arr) gives the number of elements. 3. Bounds: Accessing arr[i] with i outside [0, len(arr)-1] causes an error. 4. Contiguity: Elements are stored next to each other in memory. This is foundational for L4 because comparing elements requires accessing them via indices. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (arrays start at 0), or forgetting that len(arr) is the count, not the last index (last index is len(arr)-1). Real code pattern: arr = [10, 20, 30]; first_element = arr[0]; # 10 length = len(arr); # 3.

examples.trace: arr[0] = 5, arr[1] = 10, arr[2] = 15, len(arr) = 3, valid indices are 0,1,2.
arr[0] = 100, len(arr) = 1, only index 0 is valid.

approaches.name: Modified Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Adapt binary search to always move towards the right when the target is found, continuing until the search space is exhausted to locate the last occurrence. This ensures logarithmic time by directly targeting the last index without extra scans.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Last Occurrence of Target in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of modified binary search to find the last occurrence index of a target element in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the last occurrence of a target element in a sorted array using binary search. The key insight is that standard binary search can find any occurrence, but not necessarily the last one. We modify the binary search algorithm to continue searching towards the right even when we find the target, ensuring we locate the last occurrence. The intuition is that when we find the target at mid, we know the last occurrence must be at mid or to the right, so we move left to mid (not mid-1) to keep searching the right half. We maintain a result variable that updates whenever we find the target. The algorithm initializes left=0, right=n-1, result=-1. While left <= right, calculate mid = left + (right-left)//2. If arr[mid] == target, update result=mid and set left=mid+1 to search right half. If arr[mid] < target, set left=mid+1. If arr[mid] > target, set right=mid-1. Return result. Time complexity is O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by implementing the complete modified binary search routine. Common mistakes include not updating result correctly, infinite loops from incorrect bounds updates, and off-by-one errors in mid calculation. Real code pattern: def find_last_occurrence(arr, target): left, right, result = 0, len(arr)-1, -1; while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; left = mid+1; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=5, result=-1, mid=2, arr[2]=2==target → result=2, left=3. Step 2: left=3, right=5, mid=4, arr[4]=3>target → right=3. Step 3: left=3, right=3, mid=3, arr[3]=2==target → result=3, left=4. Step 4: left=4, right=3 → loop ends, return result=3.

Step 1: left=0, right=5, result=-1, mid=2, arr[2]=7<target → left=3. Step 2: left=3, right=5, mid=4, arr[4]=8==target → result=4, left=5. Step 3: left=5, right=5, mid=5, arr[5]=10>target → right=4. Step 4: left=5, right=4 → loop ends, return result=4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modified Binary Search for Last Occurrence
ladders.desc: The core binary search algorithm modified to always move right when target is found.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the modified binary search that finds the last occurrence of a target. The intuition is that in standard binary search, when we find the target, we return immediately, which gives any occurrence. To find the last occurrence, we need to continue searching the right half even after finding a match. We achieve this by updating a result variable when arr[mid]==target and then setting left=mid+1 to search further right. This ensures we don't stop early and exhaust the search space towards the right. The algorithm: initialize left, right, result=-1. While left<=right: calculate mid. If arr[mid]==target: update result=mid, set left=mid+1. If arr[mid]<target: left=mid+1. If arr[mid]>target: right=mid-1. Return result. Time O(log n), space O(1). This connects to L0 as the complete solution uses this routine. Common mistakes: forgetting to update result, incorrect bounds leading to missed elements, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: def modified_binary_search(arr, target): left, right, result = 0, len(arr)-1, -1; while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; left = mid+1; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3, result=-1, mid=1, arr[1]=2==target → result=1, left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, arr[2]=2==target → result=2, left=3. Step 3: left=3, right=3, mid=3, arr[3]=3>target → right=2. Step 4: left=3, right=2 → loop ends, return result=2.

Step 1: left=0, right=4, result=-1, mid=2, arr[2]=6==target → result=2, left=3. Step 2: left=3, right=4, mid=3, arr[3]=6==target → result=3, left=4. Step 3: left=4, right=4, mid=4, arr[4]=7>target → right=3. Step 4: left=4, right=3 → loop ends, return result=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Moving Right When Target Found
ladders.desc: The central technique of updating search bounds to continue towards the right after finding the target.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of the modified binary search: when we find the target at mid, we update a result variable and set left=mid+1 to search the right half. The intuition is that the last occurrence must be at or to the right of any found occurrence, so by moving left to mid+1, we narrow the search to indices greater than mid. This differs from standard binary search where we return immediately upon finding the target. The algorithm for this logic: if arr[mid]==target: result=mid; left=mid+1. This ensures we don't stop early and can locate the rightmost target. Time O(log n) as part of binary search, space O(1). This connects to L1 as it's the key modification in the sub-routine. Common mistakes: setting left=mid instead of mid+1 (could cause infinite loop), or not updating result. Real code pattern: if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; left = mid + 1.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 2, 2, 3], target = 2, current left=0, right=4, mid=2
examples.trace: At mid=2, arr[2]=2 equals target. Set result=2 to record this occurrence. Then set left=mid+1=3 to search indices 3-4 for possible later occurrences.

examples.input: arr = [4, 4, 5, 5, 5], target = 5, current left=2, right=4, mid=3
examples.trace: At mid=3, arr[3]=5 equals target. Set result=3. Set left=4 to check index 4. This ensures if arr[4] is also 5, it will be found in next iteration.

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search with Result Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement binary search that tracks and returns a result variable instead of immediate return.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves modifying standard binary search to use a result variable that gets updated during the search, rather than returning immediately when the target is found. The intuition is that by maintaining a result variable, we can continue searching and update it if we find a better match (e.g., a later occurrence). The algorithm: initialize result=-1. Perform standard binary search: while left<=right, calculate mid. If arr[mid]==target, update result=mid and proceed with search (don't return). If arr[mid]<target, left=mid+1. If arr[mid]>target, right=mid-1. After loop, return result. This teaches how to defer the return until search completes. Time O(log n), space O(1). This connects to L2 as it introduces the result tracking needed for moving right. Common mistakes: returning early, not initializing result, or incorrect update logic. Real code pattern: result = -1; while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; # continue search; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3, result=-1, mid=1, arr[1]=2==target → result=1, left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, arr[2]=2==target → result=2, left=3. Step 3: left=3, right=3, mid=3, arr[3]=3>target → right=2. Step 4: left=3, right=2 → loop ends, return result=2.
Step 1: left=0, right=3, result=-1, mid=1, arr[1]=5==target → result=1, left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, arr[2]=6>target → right=1. Step 3: left=2, right=1 → loop ends, return result=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Search Mid Calculation and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental operations of calculating mid index and comparing array element with target.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations in binary search: calculating the mid index and comparing the element at mid with the target. The intuition is that binary search relies on repeatedly checking the middle element to decide which half to search next. The mid is calculated as mid = left + (right-left)//2 to avoid overflow. Then, compare arr[mid] with target: if equal, we found the target; if less, search right half; if greater, search left half. This operation is the core of each iteration. Time O(1) per operation, space O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used in each step of the binary search with result tracking. Common mistakes: incorrect mid calculation (e.g., using (left+right)//2 which can overflow), or off-by-one in comparisons. Real code pattern: mid = left + (right - left) // 2; if arr[mid] == target: # found; elif arr[mid] < target: # search right; else: # search left.

examples.trace: Calculate mid = 0 + (5-0)//2 = 2. arr[2] = 3. Compare 3 < 4 (target), so we need to search the right half (indices 3-5).
Calculate mid = 3 + (7-3)//2 = 5. arr[5] = 12. Compare 12 > 10, so search left half (indices 3-4).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Index Access
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of sorted arrays and how to access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces sorted arrays and index-based access. A sorted array is a list of elements arranged in non-decreasing order (each element is less than or equal to the next). Index access means retrieving an element by its position, starting from 0. For example, in arr = [1, 3, 5, 7], arr[0]=1, arr[1]=3, etc. The intuition is that binary search relies on the array being sorted to efficiently narrow down the search space. Understanding indices is crucial for calculating mid and comparing elements. Time O(1) to access an element, space O(1). This connects to L4 as it provides the basic structure needed for binary search operations. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in indices, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, or assuming array is sorted when it's not. Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]; element = arr[2] # gives 3.

examples.trace: The array is sorted: 2 < 4 < 6 < 8 < 10. Index 2 refers to the third element (since indices start at 0). arr[2] = 6.
Array is sorted in non-decreasing order: 1 ≤ 1 ≤ 2 ≤ 3 ≤ 5. Index 4 is the fifth element. arr[4] = 5.
